coredata.dc:title: A novel hydrothermal approach to preparing ZnO flower-like using CQDs as growth seeds
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Blancas, Josué
Cayetano-Castro, N.
Pérez, R.
Rosas, G.
coredata.dc:description: This study presents a new synthesis route to prepare ZnO flower-like particles. Firstly, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared hydrothermally, using the Malva sylvestris flower as the sole carbon source. Subsequently, flower-like particles of ZnO/CQDs were synthesized using CQDs seeds and the same hydrothermal process. The efficiency of the redox process of solar excitation was measured by reducing the dye methylene blue (MB). The SEM and TEM characterization shows ZnO flower-like particles of around 1.5 µm whose petals are formed by crystallites of around 10 nm. ZnO nanocrystals appear embedded in an amorphous carbon matrix. TEM images show carbon quantum dots on the surface of ZnO flowers. Without using Malva sylvestris, ZnO morphologies are not formed; instead, 2D nanolaminates appear. The ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite achieved 98 % MB degradation in 80 min. The small size of CQDs can be a good scenario for the growth of crystalline nanostructures using the nanocarbon seed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO flower-like
ZnO nanosheets
CQDs
Seed-growth
Degradation
Methylene blue

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CQDs synthesized with Malva sylvestris: (a) UV–Vis spectrum, (b) corresponding band gap calculation, (c) bright-field TEM micrograph, and (d) high-resolution electron microscopy image.
SEM micrographs of the ZnO flower-type nanocomposite.
EDS chemical composition of ZnO flower-like particles, (a) EDS spectrum, and (b-f) chemical mapping of the nanoflower sample.
SEM micrographs of ZnO nanosheets forming without Malva sylvestris plant under the same experimental conditions used to form ZnO flower-like particles.
SEM micrographs of ZnONPs synthesized by soft chemical synthesis.
XRD patterns of ZnO (a) flower-like particles and (b) nanoparticles.
UV − Vis absorption spectrum of (a) ZnO/CQDs, (b) their corresponding band gap estimations, (c) Uv–vis and bandgap of ZnO nanoparticles.
Bright-field TEM micrograph (a) and SAED pattern of the ZnO flower-like particles (b).
High resolution TEM micrograph (a) of the nanoflowers and the corresponding fast Fourier pattern (b), High resolution TEM micrograph showing small crystallites having the CQDs interspacing’s (c-d).
Bright-field TEM image of the ZnO nanosheets (a), their corresponding SAED pattern (b) and a high-resolution TEM image.
Bright-field TEM image of the ZnO nanoparticles (a) and their corresponding SAED pattern (b).
FTIR spectra of CQDs (a), individual ZnO nanoparticles (b), and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites (c).
ZnO flower-like morphology plausible growth method.

Degradation graphs of methylene blue dye under solar light irradiation using (a) ZnONPs and (b) ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Different works on the ZnO flower morphology.

row.entry: Ammonia water and sodium hydroxide as precipitant

Ammonia solution, NaOH and Arginine
pH 10–11

entry.bold: Degradation of methylene blue dye with ZnO

head.ce:title: A novel hydrothermal approach to preparing ZnO flower-like using CQDs as growth seeds

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents a new synthesis route to prepare ZnO flower-like particles. Firstly, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared hydrothermally, using the Malva sylvestris flower as the sole carbon source. Subsequently, flower-like particles of ZnO/CQDs were synthesized using CQDs seeds and the same hydrothermal process. The efficiency of the redox process of solar excitation was measured by reducing the dye methylene blue (MB). The SEM and TEM characterization shows ZnO flower-like particles of around 1.5 µm whose petals are formed by crystallites of around 10 nm. ZnO nanocrystals appear embedded in an amorphous carbon matrix. TEM images show carbon quantum dots on the surface of ZnO flowers. Without using Malva sylvestris, ZnO morphologies are not formed; instead, 2D nanolaminates appear. The ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite achieved 98 % MB degradation in 80 min. The small size of CQDs can be a good scenario for the growth of crystalline nanostructures using the nanocarbon seed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data analyzed during this study are included in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoscience and nanotechnology have led to new nanomaterials that differ in morphology and quantum confinement degree from the bulk material [1]. For example, zinc oxide (ZnO) semiconductors can be prepared in different shapes, including nanoparticles [2], nanorods [3,4], nanosheets, [5] nanopencils-like [6], needles [7], and flower-like particles [8–11]. These nanostructures with different degrees of confinement ranging from 0D to 2D are synthesized using different methods, including microwaves [12] and soft chemistry [13], but mainly for hydrothermal methods [3–6,14].

The ZnO nanomaterial, with its large band gap at 3.2 eV, is a promising candidate for electron photogeneration [15]. It's also non-toxic, photochemically stable, and inexpensive [16]. However, ZnO's rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes leads to low photovoltaic conversion efficiency and decreased redox active sites [17]. Several methods have been reported to reduce charge carrier recombination using electron-harvesting agents, including ZnO combination with other materials. One such combination is ZnO with C-based nanomaterials like ZnO/MWCNTs [18–20], ZnO/GO [21–23], and ZnO/CQDs [24–26]. The ZnO/CQDs combination, with its characteristics of photoinduced electron transfer, lower cost, and water solubility, is particularly intriguing [27,28]. CQDs can be obtained through various methods, including eco-friendly ones [29]. While ZnO/CQDs composite have been synthesized into various morphologies of ZnO, including particles [30], spheres [31], rods [32,33], and flowers [34], the strategy discussed below in this document presents a novel approach.

At the nanoscale, materials' properties are amplified or altered, leading to new phenomena or enhancements of existing ones [35]. For instance, the galvanic displacement and Kirkendall effect, previously known, are now applied in the chemical transformation of nanomaterials [36]. Another example is using small particles as growth nuclei to form new nanostructures [9,37].

Table 1 summarizes studies on ZnO specifically developed for flower-like morphology. The methods to obtain this material from these studies are soft chemistry, microwaves, and hydrothermal. As can be seen, the processing times varied, although those carried out by microwaves are the shortest, followed by colloidal chemistry and finally hydrothermal. However, hydrothermal methods use the least number of reagents in the process. Precursors such as Zn(CH3COO)2*2H2O, ZnSO4*7H2O, ZnCl2, and Zn(NO3)2*6H2O are used. Due to the lateral size of the flowers, which ranges between 1 and 4 µm, they are considered micrometric. However, several researchers have pointed out that their petals are formed from nanometric crystals. For the growth of this flower-like morphology, some studies use ZnO seeds and hexagonal SiO2 substrates. However, no study has considered using quantum dots as a seed for the growth of this type of morphology. Quantum dots are also charge carriers that can enhance the flower-like morphology's redox activity.

This study exploits the small size of CQDs as growth seeds to obtain ZnO nanostructures through a one-step hydrothermal approach in ZnO/CQDs flower-like morphology. Firstly, CQDs were prepared hydrothermally, using the Malva sylvestris flower as the sole carbon source. Then, flower-like particles of ZnO/CQDs were synthesized using the same hydrothermal conditions to prepare the CQDs. The Malva sylvestris flower is native to Europe and is currently found throughout Mexico [52]. It is an annual or perennial plant with high pharmacological activity. This plant grows spontaneously and contains high-molecular-weight macromolecules that can dissolve in water. Because of this, it can be used to obtain quantum dots as a carbon sole source [53].

The results provided evidence that CQDs act as growth nuclei when forming flower-like particles. Finally, it demonstrated the potential applications of catalytic properties of the hybrid nanomaterial in the degradation of methylene blue dye as a promise for environmental remediation.

The hydrothermal process for making carbon quantum dots involved using 0.2 g of Malva sylvestris flower in a 100 mL Teflon vessel. 40 mL of deionized water was added to reach 40 % of the container's total volume. The solution was sealed in an autoclave and heated to 170 °C for 24 h in an electric commercial oven. Afterward, it was allowed to cool to room temperature.

The ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite was synthesized using the same hydrothermal procedure. However, the synthesis was conducted involving the addition of 5 ml of a 0.2 M aqueous solution of Zn(NO3)2 (Sigma—Aldrich, 98 %), 7 ml of a 1 mM NaOH solution (Meyer, 97 %), and 0.2 g of Malva sylvestris flower to a Teflon vessel. Subsequently, 28 ml of deionized water was added to make up 40 % of the total volume. The mixture was then subjected to a temperature of 170 °C for 24 h, ensuring the formation of the desired nanocomposite.

The experiment was repeated without adding the Malva sylvestris flower, resulting in the formation of ZnO nanoplates. ZnONPs were also prepared using a soft chemistry method. The 0.2 M aqueous Zn(NO3)2 solution was heated to 50 °C, and the 1 M NaOH solution was added drop by drop. The process led to the forming of a striking, white-colored solution, which was then allowed to cool to room temperature. All experiments were washed and centrifuged with deionized water and ethanol (99 %). The size and morphology of the prepared powders were analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with a BRUKER EDS attachment. The crystalline phases of the particles were identified using a BRUKER D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffraction (XRD) equipment with CuKα1 radiation. Functional groups present were determined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) using a BRUKER TENSOR 27 equipment. The crystal structure and morphology of the particles were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with the JEM-2100 equipment. Additionally, UV–Vis adsorption analyses were conducted using UV VE-5600 equipment.

A solution was prepared with 100 mg of methylene blue in 1000 ml of deionized dye water at 100 ppm. Subsequently, two 80 ml portions were taken in beakers and shaken for 5 min. Once stabilized, 10 mg of nanocomposite powder and nanoparticles were added, labeling each as ZnO/CQDs – AM and ZnONPs – AM, respectively. Once the nanomaterials were poured, they were placed in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min to stabilize the adsorption–desorption reaction of the dye on the nanomaterials. The next step was to expose the ZnO/CQDs – AM and ZnONPs – AM solutions to sunlight under the same radiation conditions. Aliquots of the solutions were taken every 20 min, and these were then centrifuged to extract any residues that the aliquots might contain and stored in closed boxes hidden from solar radiation. This process was carried out until the degradation was complete.

This research used CQDs as nucleation sites to make flower-like ZnO particles. Initially, the CQDs were produced through hydrothermal synthesis using Malva sylvestris as a carbon source. In Fig. 1 a, the UV–Vis spectrum of the CQDs is depicted, showing two absorption peaks at around 220 and 260 nm. These peaks are attributed to the n – π * and π – π * electronic transitions, which are associated with the presence of C=C and C-OH functional groups, respectively. The band gap energy of the samples was determined using the Tauc relationship (Eq. (1) [35,36] by plotting the curve of (αhν)2 against the photon energy (hν), as illustrated in Fig. 1b.
(1) (αhν)n = A(hν-Eg)
The measured band gap energy values are Eg = 3.9 eV for C and 4.6 eV for C-OH. These results provide evidence for the existence of CQDs.

Fig. 1c displays a bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image showing small particles of less than 5 nm. The high-resolution image (Fig. 1d) reveals lattice fringes of approximately 3.6 Å and 2.4 Å, which are close to the equivalent distance to the (002) and (100) planes of the hexagonal carbon structure, demonstrates the formation of CQDs using Malva sylvestris.

In the second step, the same hydrothermal reaction strategy was used to produce carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as was used to create ZnO particles using the precursor, NaOH, and Malva sylvestris. SEM micrographs in Fig. 2 a-b display the synthesis of hierarchical and uniform ZnO flower-like particles, with an average size of about 1.5 μm and arms measuring between 300 and 400 nm. In image 2c, the porous and rough petals may be important for their catalytic properties [3]. Fig. S1 shows SEM images of another view of these ZnO flower-like particles.

EDS analysis of these particles (Fig. 3 a) indicates the presence of Zn, O, and C. Additionally, Fig. 3b-f confirm the chemical composition, with EDS elemental chemical mapping verifying the spatial distribution of the three elements in the microstructure. These results suggest that the flower-like particles comprise Zn, O, and C.

In the third step, we experimented to investigate the potential impact of CQDs on the formation of flower-like particles without using Malva sylvestris. The SEM images in Fig. 4 a-c display the distinct morphology obtained compared to using the Malva sylvestris plant. The images show nanosheets with an average size of around 0.5 µm. These materials exhibit good transparency due to their nanometric thickness, classifying them as 2D nanostructures. The thin appearance of these nanosheets may be attributed to the high pressure generated during the hydrothermal process. The EDS chemical analysis in Fig. 4d reveals the presence of only Zn and O elements corresponding to the observed particles. These results indicate that using the Malva sylvestris plant facilitated the formation of ZnO flower-like particles.

This study also prepared ZnO nanoparticles using soft chemistry methods to compare them with ZnO flower-like particles while evaluating their catalytic properties. The SEM micrographs in Fig. 5 a-c show the particles at different magnifications. Fig. 5a-b displays a spherical ZnO aggregate of approximately 100 nm. On the other hand, in subsection (c), it becomes apparent that the aggregates consist of nanoparticles smaller than 20 nm. Fig. S2 shows the chemical analysis and elemental mapping of the elements Zn and O present in the sample.

Fig. 6 a-b shows the XRD pattern having angular positions in 47.74°, 56.80°, 63.15°, 66.63°, 68.23° and 69.36°, which correspond to the diffraction of the planes (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112) and (201) of the crystal structure of the wurtzite-type hexagonal ZnO. The space group and lattice parameters are P63mc (186) with a = 3.2417 Å, c = 5.1876 Å, indexed according to data card No. 01 079 0205 (JCPDS). Using the Williamson-Hall method, the size of the ZnO crystallites was determined to be 10 nm. These nanocrystals indicate the formation of nanostructured ZnO and suggest that the ZnO flower petals grow without a specific direction or in a single-crystalline mode. At the same time, the XRD pattern shows noticeable differences in the half-width of the (101) peak (FWHM=0.837) compared to the (002) planes (FWHM=0.449). Additionally, the intensity of the (002) planes indicates a preferential orientation, suggesting greater crystallization along this direction. This preference was confirmed by comparing the intensity ratio of (101) to (002), which was expected to be 1.315 but was measured at 2.419. These findings suggest epitaxial growth of ZnO in the (002) planes, possibly due to the hexagonal structure with preferential orientation caused by the presence of CQDs. The results confirm the formation of flower-like ZnO particles. However, the XRD pattern did not show a signal of the CQDs or C, possibly due to the lower amount relative to ZnO and its lower atomic number compared to C. These results broadly align with previous XRD studies of similar materials [30,33,34].

Fig. 7 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of flower-like ZnO particles. The spectrum exhibits absorption bands at 210 nm and 276 nm, which are attributed to the presence of the CQDs in the sample. There is also an absorption peak at 361 nm with continuous absorption in the visible range of flower-like ZnO particles. Notably, the peak shows a blue shift compared to other works on flower-like ZnO [24,37], indicating a unique feature of our ZnO particles. These results strongly suggest that the flower-like ZnO particles contain a phase mixture of ZnO and carbon nanostructures.

The Tauc ratio was employed for band gap determination [35,36], which provides a key parameter to understand the optical properties of flower-shaped ZnO particles. The band gap energy of the sample determination is shown in Fig. 7b. The measured band gap was 3.9 for flower-shaped ZnO particles and 3.2 eV for ZnONP (Fig. 7c). These values ​​agree with other works on flower-shaped ZnO particles [24,37].

The UV–vis data regarding CQDs and ZnO could be further supported by performing transmission electron microscopy (TEM) studies. Fig. 8 a shows a bright-field TEM image of a petal of the flower-shaped ZnO particles. The image shows a nanostructured ZnO material of many crystallites with an average size of around 10 nm. These crystallites are embedded in an organic matrix on the surface. Therefore, the arms of the flower-shaped particle are polycrystalline, with organic substances potentially contributing to their growth. Additionally, Fig. 8b displays the corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern, where the same reflections observed in the XRD pattern appear in the rings corresponding to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), and (201) crystallographic planes. The distribution of these planes aligns with the crystal structure of hexagonal ZnO. Once again, no reflections from CQDs were observed in this SAED pattern, as noted previously in the XRD discussion.

Since the Malva sylvestris flower has large petals, existing binders could be involved in the growth process below discussed. Furthermore, these findings explain the roughness and porosity of the petals observed in the 3D SEM images.

Fig. 9 a presents a high-resolution TEM image of the material, revealing several ZnO crystallites below 10 nm. The crystallites of the nanostructured material exhibit a classic random misorientation. In Fig. 9b, the corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern displays diffraction spots from the main crystallographic planes of the hexagonal structure of ZnO, including (100), (002), and (101). However, some spots are attributed to the presence of (002) planes of CQDs, with the carbon matrix observed in the background. Additionally, TEM analysis reveals particles with interplanar distances corresponding to CQDs (Fig. 9c-d) on the material's surface. At the top of the image, an interesting feature commonly observed in carbon nanostructures can be seen. These results, agreeing with the X-ray diffraction analysis, provide valuable insight into the characteristics of our nanocomposites.

Fig. 10 a display a bright field TEM image and its corresponding SAED pattern of the ZnO nanosheets. This image confirms the sheet-like morphology observed by SEM, which was obtained through hydrothermal treatment without Malva sylvestris. Once again, the transparency of the sheets indicates they have a very small thickness, measured in nanometers. We have also found rod-like shapes using this technique. The electron diffraction pattern in Fig. 10 b displays the main indexed planes of the hexagonal structure of ZnO. The pattern shows distinct spots and rings, suggesting that the sample has a specific orientation or texture. This discovery is supported by the high-resolution image in Fig. 10c, where the fast Fourier transform (FFT) reveals the presence of the (002) reflection, which represents the most intense planes (100 %). The axis is perpendicular to the lattice fringes, indicating that the plates grow in the [002] direction, which makes sense considering that the “c” lattice parameter of hexagonal ZnO is greater than the “a” lattice parameter, clearly showing anisotropic growth.

Fig. 11 a shows the bright field of ZnO nanoparticles produced using soft chemistry. The nanoparticles were directly measured to oscillate in the 20–50 nm range. The SAED (Fig. 11b) diffraction pattern illustrates the hexagonal structure of ZnO. The continuity of the rings in this pattern is greater than that of the previous figure, indicating the presence of smaller and disoriented crystals.

Fig. 12 a-c shows the FTIR spectra of the CQDs, single ZnO nanoparticles, and ZnO/CQD nanocomposites, respectively. A broad absorption band near 3410 cm−1 is observed in all spectra, assigned to the O–H stretching vibrations. A peak at 1645 cm−1 of the aromatic groups can be attributed to the C=C bond in the spectrum of CQDs. However, the nanocomposite's spectrum shows a new peak near 900 cm-1, confirming the Zn–O–C interaction. In the spectrum of nanoparticles and nanocomposites, two typical characteristic peaks appear at 436 and 553 cm−1, corresponding to the Zn-O bond of ZnO. However, the nanocomposite's spectrum shows a new peak near 900 cm−1, confirming the Zn–O–C interaction. These bands, commonly described in nanostructures including the flower-like ZnO [36,37], assure that the carboxyl group of the CQDs successfully reacted with the hydroxyl group of ZnO during the reaction process. Furthermore, they confirm the Malva sylvestris flower's active participation in forming the flower-like ZnO nanostructure.

Based on the facts above, a potential mechanism for forming ZnO's flower-like morphology is proposed (see Fig. 13 ). The mechanism for the sole ZnO flower growth has been previously discussed in several articles [40,41,44]. However, the role of carbon quantum dots as active nucleation sites is significant. Because their small size and hexagonal structure, which is compatible with the growth of the hexagonal structure of ZnO along the [001] direction. ZnO flowers grow on a hexagonal substrate based on hexagonal Si [38]. Therefore, it is plausible that these carbon particles become growth seeds, as previously seen when ZnO NPs seeds are used [42]. Based on the findings in Fig. 8, which show that ZnO crystals are clustered in an amorphous structure, we need to consider how this affects the processing of the long-chain substances found in the Malva sylvestris flower. The molecules in the Malva sylvestris flower are likely ligands that influence the specific morphology in which ZnO crystals develop.

The plausible growth mechanism is based on the nucleation and growth theories. First, the zinc oxide atoms are reduced from the precursor salt (Fig. 13a) and diffuse to form the ZnO particles, which grow by heterogeneous nucleation on the CQDs (Fig. 13b) in such a way that the nucleation is favored to form many growth nuclei, which explains the nanometric sizes of the ZnO crystals (Fig. 13c). In the second stage, the residues of the organic molecules existing in Malva sylvestris that have been dissolved allow random growth, and their ligands existing help to develop a flower-like microstructure of these growth sites in which the petals wrap the multiple nanometric reduced ZnO crystallites (Fig. 13d). Like a floccule’s shaped like petals that make up the complex growth and aggregation of the flower petals (Fig. 13e). Subsequently, the growth follows Oswald's ripening (Fig. 13f). It has been commented that adding NaOH to the system favors an alkaline pH suitable for developing the flower-like morphology [41,42,45–51].

Fig. 14 a-b shows the degradation spectra of MB dye under sunlight using ZnONPs and ZnO/CQDs materials. The degradation reaction took 80 min with ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite and 120 min with ZnONPs. ZnO/CQDs achieved a 98 % degradation efficiency, while ZnONPs achieved 97 %. After degradation, MB dye turned into leucomethylene, a less harmful substance [54]. The enhanced performance in degrading organic dyes is attributed to the carbon matrix and CQDs, causing electron leakage. Other studies have also demonstrated the efficiency of these materials in dye reduction, as indicated in Table 2 . For example, our results were superior to those obtained using flower-like particles without CQDs [16] and like the results obtained using another carbon nanostructure [55], highlighting the degradation efficiency of the ZnO/CQDs material in its nanocomposite and nanostructured form.

With the energy of sunlight surpassing the band gap energy of ZnO, it excites the material, producing the separation of charge carriers (electrons and holes). The e- in the conduction band interact with oxygen (O2) molecules adsorbed on ZnO generating superoxide anion radicals and the resulting holes produce hydroxyl radicals (OH) [54]. However, this generation of electron-hole pairs has a fast recombination rate. The incorporation of CQDs improves the separation of photogenerated charge carriers and increases the recombination time by extracting and transporting the electron-hole pairs to the surface [56]. The charges move to the surface of the material and react with O2 and H2O molecules in the solution to form O−2 and OH radicals. Finally, these highly oxidizing radicals will approach the adsorbed MB and mineralize it into CO2, H2O, etc. [58].

In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were produced using a hydrothermal method with the flower of Malva sylvestris as a carbon source. Subsequently, a CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite was made using the same approach, and the necessary reagents to form the ZnO were added, forming flower-like particles. The objective was to achieve a ZnO flower seed-based growth of CQDs. The efficiency of the solar excitation redox process in the nanocomposite was evaluated by reducing methylene blue dye. The materials were analyzed using SEM-EDS, TEM, XRD, FTIR, and UV–vis techniques, confirming the successful synthesis of CQDs with sizes ranging from 1-6 µm using the flower of Malva sylvestris. Also, chemical and structural characterization indicated the formation of flower-like particles in the CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite, with measurements indicating a size of around 1.5 µm. This flower-like morphology consists of nanostructured petals with crystallites of approximately 10 nm embedded in an amorphous carbon matrix. The ZnO petals were found to be polycrystalline, and their growth was linked to the organic components of the Malva sylvestris flower. Some carbon quantum dots on the surface were also identified following TEM analysis. Notably, without using the Malva sylvestris flower, a different morphology composed of small 2D nanolaminates was observed during the same hydrothermal process. The ZnO nanoparticles achieved 97 % methylene blue (MB) degradation in 120 min, while the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite exhibited improved performance, reaching 98 % degradation in 80 min. These results were attributed to the presence of an amorphous carbon matrix and CQDs, with the small size being beneficial for the growth of crystalline nanostructures using a nanocarbon seed.

Josué Blancas: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. N. Cayetano-Castro: Methodology. R. Pérez: Visualization. G. Rosas: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author Josue Blancas thanks the financial support to CONAHCyT and the Research Institute in Metallurgy and Materials (UMSNH) to provide facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2024.117654.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: ZnO nanostructured materials and their potential applications: progress, challenges and perspectives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Raha, Md. Ahmaruzzaman, ZnO nanostructured materials and their potential applications: progress, challenges and perspectives, Nanoscale Adv 4 (2022) 1868–1925. https://doi.org/10.1039/D1NA00880C.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structure-directing property and growth mechanism induced by capping agents in nanostructured ZnO during hydrothermal synthesis—A systematic review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Basnet, S.
